question.name: 1 Text Answer
question.text:
5.1 Text Answer

  • (a)

  • (b)

  • (c)

Define (unambiguously) a fluid.

<1>

Help

A substance that deforms continuously under the application of shear stress

Fluids are all around you in everyday life, give examples.

<2>

Help

Show Answer

An instructor-led video working through the problem, auto-pausing when it's your turn to work. Good if you don't know where to start, or are stuck half-way through.
Check your own answer before revealing the answer. Revealing the answer takes away the chance to get it right yourself.
Only view worked solutions after making your best independent attempt. Seeing solutions ruins the learning opportunity. If you are stuck half-way, try (hard!) to only reveal necessary steps before continuing independently.

Air, water, yogurt, petrol, diesel, oil, natural gas(methane), beer, wine, beverages in general, helium, treacle, blood, sweat, tears, sperm, snake poison, vacinations, etc

How do the fluids identified above differ? Contrast by comparing their physical properties, e.g.viscosity, density. [Give units in each case]

<3>

Help

Show Answer

An instructor-led video working through the problem, auto-pausing when it's your turn to work. Good if you don't know where to start, or are stuck half-way through.
Check your own answer before revealing the answer. Revealing the answer takes away the chance to get it right yourself.
Only view worked solutions after making your best independent attempt. Seeing solutions ruins the learning opportunity. If you are stuck half-way, try (hard!) to only reveal necessary steps before continuing independently.

- density differ: \([\rho ]=\frac{M}{{L}^{3}}\)
- viscosity differ: \([\mathcal{M}]=\frac{M}{LT}\)
- composition differs: e.g. air is composed of nitrogen(78%), oxygen(21%), Argon(0.4%), Carbon Dioxide(0.04%), etc.

question.name: 2 Multiple Choice (Q2.2)
question.text:
5.2 Multiple Choice (Q2.2)

A stagnant reservoir is stably stratified with two immiscible liquids of density \(\rho_{1}\)and \(\rho_{2}\) where \(\rho_{1} > \rho_{2}\), respectively. Which graph below depicts correctly the distribution of gauge pressure along a vertical line through the liquids.

<1>

Help

An instructor-led video working through the problem, auto-pausing when it's your turn to work. Good if you don't know where to start, or are stuck half-way through.

question.name: 3 Identify The Mistakes (Q4.2)
question.text:
5.3 Identify The Mistakes (Q4.2)

Gas is transported in a horizontal pipeline that has a change in cross-sectional area as depicted. One of the engineers working on the project drew up the following diagram for right to left flow within the pipeline. What, if anything, is wrong with it?

<1>

Help

choices.choice: The eddies are in the wrong direction
Streamlines cannot come from a wall
Streamlines cannot be drawn along walls

question.name: 4 Identify The Mistakes 2 (Q4.2)
question.text:

The following diagram represents left to right flow within a gas pipeline. What, if anything, is wrong with it?

question.name: 5 Algorithmic Answer (Q6.2)
question.text:
5.5 Algorithmic Answer (Q6.2)

A hydraulic jump is in place downstream of a spillway as shown below. Upstream of the jump, the depth of the stream is $d m and the average flow velocity is $u m/s. Just downstream of the jump, the average velocity is $v m/s. Calculate the water depth h just downstream of the jump. Give your answer to 3 significant figures.

<1>

Help

Show Answer

Worked Solutions

An instructor-led video working through the problem, auto-pausing when it's your turn to work. Good if you don't know where to start, or are stuck half-way through.
Check your own answer before revealing the answer. Revealing the answer takes away the chance to get it right yourself.
Only view worked solutions after making your best independent attempt. Seeing solutions ruins the learning opportunity. If you are stuck half-way, try (hard!) to only reveal necessary steps before continuing independently.
⬆ Previous Step ⬆
⬇ Next Step ⬇
Show All

Assume the spillway has width w. Volume conservation in CV:
\(\frac{d V}{d t}=Q_{1}-Q_{2}=u_{1} h_{1} w-u_{2} h_{2} w\)

Hydraulic jump is in place so V=constant :
\(\therefore Q_{1}=Q_{2} \Rightarrow u_{1} h_{1}=u_{2} h_{2}\)

Thus, \(h_{2}=\frac{u_{1}}{u_{2}} h_{1}\)

$h m

$h m

Q5 Comments

View/make comments; ask/answer questions. Give feedback on the questions to help us improve. Comments are anonymous.

question.name: 6 Algorithmic Answer 2 (Q7.2)
question.text:
5.6 Algorithmic Answer 2 (Q7.2)

The flow in a rectangular open channel (up-stream depth \(h_{1}=\) $h1 m and channel width $w m) passes under a vertical plate sluice gate. The volume flow rate Q=$Q \(m^{3}/s\) and the downstream depth \(h_{2}\)=$h2 m

  • (a)

  • (b)

Draw a diagram of the flow showing an appropriate control volume and the forces acting on the fluid. Note the pressure distribution is hydrostatic sufficiently far away from the sluice gate (why is the flow near the sluice gate not hydrostatic?)

<1>

Help

Show Answer

Worked Solutions

An instructor-led video working through the problem, auto-pausing when it's your turn to work. Good if you don't know where to start, or are stuck half-way through.
Check your own answer before revealing the answer. Revealing the answer takes away the chance to get it right yourself.
Only view worked solutions after making your best independent attempt. Seeing solutions ruins the learning opportunity. If you are stuck half-way, try (hard!) to only reveal necessary steps before continuing independently.

Streamlines are curved near the gate, therefore the pressure is not hydrostatic.

Using the momentum equation, calculate the force on the gate. Give your answer to 3 significant figures.

<2>

Help

Show Answer

Worked Solutions

An instructor-led video working through the problem, auto-pausing when it's your turn to work. Good if you don't know where to start, or are stuck half-way through.
Check your own answer before revealing the answer. Revealing the answer takes away the chance to get it right yourself.
Only view worked solutions after making your best independent attempt. Seeing solutions ruins the learning opportunity. If you are stuck half-way, try (hard!) to only reveal necessary steps before continuing independently.

Applying momentum conservation over the CV:
\(\rho Q\left(u_{2}-u_{1}\right)=F_{p_{1}}-F_{p_{2}}-F_{G}\)

⬆ Previous Step ⬆
⬇ Next Step ⬇
Show All

Hydrostatic pressure distributions:
\(F_{p 1}=w h_{1} \cdot \rho_{g} \frac{h_{1}}{2}, \quad F_{p 2}=w h_{2} \cdot \rho g \frac{h_{2}}{2}\)

Using continuity \(Q=u_{1} w h_{1}=u_{2} w h_{2}\), we can now calculate the Force

$F N

$F N

Q6 Comments

View/make comments; ask/answer questions. Give feedback on the questions to help us improve. Comments are anonymous.

answers.answer: ~~~~~~~~~~Check the help box to get the answer~~~~~~~~~~

question.name: 7 Algorithmic Answer 3 (Q9.4)
question.text:
5.7 Algorithmic Answer 3 (Q9.4)

You are asked to design a pipeline between two reservoirs, of which the first is $h m higher than the second. The reservoirs are $s km far apart and should carry a discharge of $Q \(m^{3}/s\). Calculate the design diameter D of the pipe. Assume that the pipe roughness is such that the friction coefficient f=0.02. Hint: neglect the exit loss. Verify afterwards if thisassumption was justified.

<1>

Help

Show Answer

Worked Solutions

An instructor-led video working through the problem, auto-pausing when it's your turn to work. Good if you don't know where to start, or are stuck half-way through.
Check your own answer before revealing the answer. Revealing the answer takes away the chance to get it right yourself.
Only view worked solutions after making your best independent attempt. Seeing solutions ruins the learning opportunity. If you are stuck half-way, try (hard!) to only reveal necessary steps before continuing independently.
⬆ Previous Step ⬆
⬇ Next Step ⬇
Show All

Apply Bernoulli along a streamline between 1 and 3, including frictional losses but neglecting the exit loss:
\(H_{1}=H_{3}+\Delta H_{1}=H_{3}+f \frac{U_{2}^{2}}{2 g} \frac{L}{D}\)

\(\overbrace{H_{1}-H_{3}}^{\Delta H}=\frac{U_{2}^{2}}{2 g} f \frac{L}{D}\)

Using that \(Q=U_{2} A_{2}=U_{2} \frac{1}{4} \pi D^{2}\) and thus \(U_{2}=\frac{4 Q}{\pi D^{2}}\) results in:
\(\Delta H=\frac{16 f L Q^{2}}{2 \pi^{2} g D^{5}}\)

\(D=\sqrt[5]{\frac{8 f L Q^{2}}{\pi^{2} g \Delta H}}\)

$d m

$d m

Q7 Comments

View/make comments; ask/answer questions. Give feedback on the questions to help us improve. Comments are anonymous.

question.name: 8 Symbolic Equations (Q1.6/7)
question.text:
5.8 Symbolic Equations (Q1.6/7)

Using volume integrals, determine the mass of a fluid of constant desity \(\rho\) in the following containers filled up to the top. Determine the integrals by summing thin slices of height d\(z\) over the \(z\)-direction.

  • (a)

  • (b)

  • (c)

The triangular prism:

m =

<1>

Help

Code

rho
Symbol

\(\rho\)

Equation Help

Show Answer

Worked Solutions

An instructor-led video working through the problem, auto-pausing when it's your turn to work. Good if you don't know where to start, or are stuck half-way through.
Check your own answer before revealing the answer. Revealing the answer takes away the chance to get it right yourself.
Only view worked solutions after making your best independent attempt. Seeing solutions ruins the learning opportunity. If you are stuck half-way, try (hard!) to only reveal necessary steps before continuing independently.

How to input expressions

The equation questions use Maple syntax to evaluate expressions. As with Python, basic operators use the symbols  + - * / ^ and parentheses can be used to structure your expressions. Some common functions can also be used such as sqrt(),  sin() and arcsin(). To get the natural exponential, use exp() as e is not intepreted as Euler's number.

For Greek letters, type the full name of the letter. For example mu*U  will render \( \mu U \) (there is no need to use a back slash).

Click the to preview your expressions.

For example, writing exp(i*pi) - 1 will produce:

\( \text{e}^{i \pi} - 1 \)

The volume can be determined by: \(V=\int_{D}^{}dV\)

⬆ Previous Step ⬆
⬇ Next Step ⬇
Show All

Taking a horizontal slice of thickness \(dz\):
\(dV=WB(z)dz\)

Using convergence of triangles :
\(\frac{B(z)}{z}=\frac{b}{h}\)

The mass can be determined by:
\(m=\int_{D}^{}dm\), where \(dm\) is element of mass defined by \(dm=\rho dV\)

Since \(\rho\) is constant , \(m=\rho\int_{D}^{}dV=\rho V\)

Substitute in volume equation from earlier

\(m=\frac{\rho}{2}wbh\)

\(m=\frac{\rho}{2}wbh\)

The truncated cone:

\(m=\)
<2>
\(\{ \frac{1}{3} \pi \)
<3>
\(^{2}h+\frac{1}{3} \pi h R_{t} \)
<4>
\(+\frac{1}{3} \pi \)
<5>
\(R_{t}^{2}\}\)

Help

Show Answer

Worked Solutions

An instructor-led video working through the problem, auto-pausing when it's your turn to work. Good if you don't know where to start, or are stuck half-way through.
Check your own answer before revealing the answer. Revealing the answer takes away the chance to get it right yourself.
Only view worked solutions after making your best independent attempt. Seeing solutions ruins the learning opportunity. If you are stuck half-way, try (hard!) to only reveal necessary steps before continuing independently.

Again consider a horizontal slice of thickness \(dz\). An element of volume is given by:
\(dV=\pi R(z)^{2}dz\)

⬆ Previous Step ⬆
⬇ Next Step ⬇
Show All

To find R(z) we use that \(R(0)=R_{0}\) and \(R(h)=R_{t}\) and the fact that R grows linearly, implying that:
\(R(z)=R_{0}+\frac{R_{t}-R_{0}}{h} z\)

The volume can be determined by:
\(\begin{aligned}V &=\pi \int_{0}^{h}\left\{\frac{R_{t}-R_{0}}{h} z+R_{0} \right\}^{2} d z \\&=\pi \int_{0}^{h}\left\{\left(\frac{R_{t}-R_{0}}{h}\right)^{2} z^{2}+2 \frac{R_{0}}{h}\left(R_{t}-R_{0}\right) z+R_{0}^{2}\right\} d z \\&=\pi\left\{\frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{R_{t}-R_{0}}{h}\right)^{2} z^{3}+\frac{R_{0}}{h}\left(R_{t}-R_{0}\right) z^{2}+\left.R_{0}^{2} z\right|_{0} ^{h}\right\} \\V &=\frac{1}{3} \pi R_{0}^{2} h+\frac{1}{3} \pi h R_{t} R_{0}+\frac{1}{3} \pi h R_{t}^{2} \quad \cdots \quad E q .(3)\end{aligned}\)

Multiplying Eq(3) with \(\frac{R_{t}-R_{0}}{R_{t}-R_{0}}\) gives us a different form of the equation:
\(\begin{aligned}&V=\frac{1}{3} \pi R_{t}^{2} h\left(\frac{R_{t}}{R_{t-} R_{0}}\right)-\frac{1}{3} \pi R_{0}^{2} h\left(\frac{R_{0}}{R_{t}-R_{0}}\right) \\&V=\frac{1}{3} \pi R_{t}^{2}\left\{h+\frac{h R_{0}}{R_{t}-R_{0}}\right\}-\frac{1}{3} \pi R_{0}^{2}\left\{\frac{h}{R_{t}-R_{t}}\right\} \quad \cdots \quad E q .(4)\end{aligned}\)

This equation (4) is equivalent to the subtraction of volume of cone 1 with volume of cone 2
\(\begin{aligned}&V_{\text {cone } 1}=\int_{-h^{*}}^{h^{*}+h} \pi R(z)^{2} d z \\&V_{\text {cone } 2}=\int_{-h^{*}}^{0} \pi R(z)^{2} d z, \text { where } h^{*}=\frac{h R_{0}}{R_{t}-R_{0}}\end{aligned}\)

In the same way to determine the mass of the fluid in the container of problem 6a and as the density of the fluid is constant, the mass is given by:
\(m=\rho \int d v\)

\(m=\rho\left\{\frac{1}{3} \pi R_{0}{ }^{2} h+\frac{1}{3} \pi h R_{t} R_{0}+\frac{1}{3} \pi h R_{t}^{2}\right\}\)

\(V=\frac{1}{3} \pi R_{t}^{2}\left\{h+\frac{h R_{0}}{R_{t}-R_{0}}\right\}-\frac{1}{3} \pi R_{0}^{2}\left\{\frac{h}{R t-R_{i}}\right\} \\ m=\rho\left\{\frac{1}{3} \pi R_{0}{ }^{2} h+\frac{1}{3} \pi h R_{t} R_{0}+\frac{1}{3} \pi h R_{t}^{2}\right\}\)

Using the geometries of the previous question, calculate the mass of the fluid if the fluid density varies linearly according to \(\rho(z)=\rho_{0}-a z\)

<6>

Help

Show Answer

Worked Solutions

An instructor-led video working through the problem, auto-pausing when it's your turn to work. Good if you don't know where to start, or are stuck half-way through.
Check your own answer before revealing the answer. Revealing the answer takes away the chance to get it right yourself.
Only view worked solutions after making your best independent attempt. Seeing solutions ruins the learning opportunity. If you are stuck half-way, try (hard!) to only reveal necessary steps before continuing independently.

If the density of the fluid varies linearly according to :
\(\rho(z)=\rho_{0}-a z\)

⬆ Previous Step ⬆
⬇ Next Step ⬇
Show All

a) The weight of the fluid in the container of problem 6. a is given as:
\(\begin{aligned}W &=\int \rho(z) g d V \\&=\int_{0}^{h}\left(\rho_{0}-a z\right) g \frac{b w}{h} z d z \\&= g w b h \left\{\frac{1}{2} \rho_{0} z^{2}-\left.\frac{a}{3} z^{3}\right|_{0} ^{h}\right\} \\W &=g w b h\left\{\frac{1}{2} \rho_{0}-\frac{a}{3} h\right\} \text { where a is the gravitational acceleration }\end{aligned}\)

b) The weight of the fluid in the container of problem 6.b is given as:
\(\begin{aligned}W &=\int_{0}^{h}\left(\rho_{0}-a z\right) g \pi\left\{\frac{R_{t}-R_{0}}{h} z+R_{0}\right\}^{2} d z \\&=\rho_{0} g \pi \int_{0}^{h}\left\{\left(\frac{R_{t}-R_{0}}{h}\right)^{2} z^{2}+2 \frac{R_{0}}{h}\left(R_{t}-R_{0}\right) z+R_{0}^{2}\right\} d z\\&-a g \pi \int_{0}^{h}\left\{\left(\frac{R_{t}-R_{0}}{h}\right)^{2} z^{3}+2 \frac{R_{0}}{h}\left(R_{t}-R_{0}\right) z^{2}+R_{0}^{2} z\right\} d z\\&=\rho_{0} g \pi\left\{\left(\frac{R_{t}-R_{0}}{h}\right)^{2} \frac{1}{3} z^{3}+\frac{R_{0}}{h}\left(R_{t}-R_{0}\right) z^{2}+\left.R_{0}^{2} z\right|_{0} ^{h}\right\}\&-a g \pi\left\{\frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{R_{t}-R_{0}}{h}\right)^{2} z^{4}+\frac{2}{3} \frac{R_{0}}{h}\left(R_{t}-R_{0}\right) z^{3}+\left.\frac{R_{0}^{2}}{2} z^{2}\right|_{0} ^{h}\right\}\&W=\rho_{0} g \pi h\left\{\frac{1}{3} R_{t}^{2}+\frac{1}{3} R_{t} R_{0}+\frac{1}{3} R_{0}^{2}\right\}-a g \pi h^{2}\left\{\frac{1}{4} R_{t}^{2}+\frac{1}{6} R_{t} R_{0}+\frac{1}{12} R_{0}^{2}\right\}\end{aligned}\)

\(W=\frac{1}{3} \rho_{0} g \pi h\left\{\left(R_{t}+R_{0}\right)^{2}-R_{t} R_{0}\right\}-\frac{1}{4} \operatorname{ag\pi h}^{2}\left\{\left(R_{t}+R_{0}\right)^{2}-\frac{2}{3}\left(R_{t} R_{0}+R_{0}^{2}\right)\right\}\)

\(W=\frac{1}{3} \rho_{0} g \pi h\left\{\left(R_{t}+R_{0}\right)^{2}-R_{t} R_{0}\right\}-\frac{1}{4} \operatorname{ag\pi h}^{2}\left\{\left(R_{t}+R_{0}\right)^{2}-\frac{2}{3}\left(R_{t} R_{0}+R_{0}^{2}\right)\right\}\)

Q8 Comments

View/make comments; ask/answer questions. Give feedback on the questions to help us improve. Comments are anonymous.

question.name: 9 Matrix Response (Q10.2)
question.text:
5.9 Matrix Response (Q10.2)

You wish to pump water from reservoir A to reservoir B. Reservoir B is 10m higher than reservoir A. The reservoirs are 200m apart, the pipe diameter is 20cm and the friction coefficient f=0.02. You have a 5kW pump which has an efficiency of 85%. You want to calculate the discharge Q. In order to calculate a solution, you can make a guess for the head loss terms, after which the discharge Q can be calculated. With this estimation of Q we can improve our guess for the head loss term. If we repeat this procedure often enough, the guessed head loss will become identical to the refined guess.

Complete the following table:

IterationGuess \(\Delta H_{f}[m]\)\(Q[m^{3}/s]\)\(U[m/s]\)Refined Guess
1<1><2><3><4>
2<5><6><7><8>
3<9><10><11><12>
4<13><14><15><16>
5<17><18><19><20>
6<21><22><23><24>

An instructor-led video working through the problem, auto-pausing when it's your turn to work. Good if you don't know where to start, or are stuck half-way through.
Check your own answer before revealing the answer. Revealing the answer takes away the chance to get it right yourself.
Only view worked solutions after making your best independent attempt. Seeing solutions ruins the learning opportunity. If you are stuck half-way, try (hard!) to only reveal necessary steps before continuing independently.
Q9 Comments

View/make comments; ask/answer questions. Give feedback on the questions to help us improve. Comments are anonymous.

question.name: 10 Combined Question
question.text:
5.10 Combined Question

Las Vegas is a city that is growing quickly. Its main supply of water is Lake Meade, but this lake will be unable to provide all of the city's water in the near future. The government is currently looking for alternatives. One such alternative is to use pump groundwater from Eastern Nevada over a large distance (540 km) to the city. It is estimated that the pipeline will need to provide 1.04 x 108 m3 of water per year. The pipe has a diameter of 1.8 m and a friction factor f=0.01. For simplicity, it may be assumed that there are no differences in elevation over the entire length of the pipe.

  • (a)

  • (b)

An A-level student states that when a fluid flow experiences friction inside a pipe it must slow down on its way through the pipe. What, if anything, is the wrong with the studentâ€™s thinking?

<1>

Help

Show Answer

Worked Solutions

An instructor-led video working through the problem, auto-pausing when it's your turn to work. Good if you don't know where to start, or are stuck half-way through.
Check your own answer before revealing the answer. Revealing the answer takes away the chance to get it right yourself.
Only view worked solutions after making your best independent attempt. Seeing solutions ruins the learning opportunity. If you are stuck half-way, try (hard!) to only reveal necessary steps before continuing independently.

By conservation of mass, it follows for a pipe of constant cross-sectional area that:
\(\rho A U_{in}=\rho A U_{out}\)

⬆ Previous Step ⬆
⬇ Next Step ⬇
Show All

For constant density flow, we have \(U_{in} = U_{out}\); and it is therefore impossible for the flow to slow down inside of the pipe.

The velocity into a pipe must be the same as the velocity out of the pipe

The energy head at section 2 is H2 = 10 m and minor losses may be ignored. Plot the energy grade line, giving the values at appropriate locations. Explain why there is little added value in plotting the hydraulic grade line.

<2>

Help

Show Answer

Worked Solutions

An instructor-led video working through the problem, auto-pausing when it's your turn to work. Good if you don't know where to start, or are stuck half-way through.
Check your own answer before revealing the answer. Revealing the answer takes away the chance to get it right yourself.
Only view worked solutions after making your best independent attempt. Seeing solutions ruins the learning opportunity. If you are stuck half-way, try (hard!) to only reveal necessary steps before continuing independently.

Pipe cross-sectional area::\(A=\frac{\pi}{4} D^{2}=2.5 m^{2}\)

⬆ Previous Step ⬆
⬇ Next Step ⬇
Show All

\(\begin{aligned} Q &=1.04 x 10^{8} \mathrm{~m}^{3} / \mathrm{yr} \\&=\frac{1.04 x 10^{8}}{365 \cdot 3600 \cdot 24}=3.3 \mathrm{~m}^{3} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}\)

\(U=\frac{Q}{A}=1.3^{\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}}\)

\(f \frac{L}{D} \frac{U^{3}}{2 g}=0.01 \cdot \frac{540 \cdot 10^{3}}{1 \cdot 8} \frac{(1.3)^{2}}{2 \cdot 9.81}=257 m\)

\(H_{1}=H_{2}+f \frac{L}{D} \frac{U^{2}}{2 g}=10+257=267 \mathrm{~m}\)

Velocity head: \(\frac{U^{2}}{2 g}=0.09 m\)

\(\therefore H_{1} = h_{1} \text{and} \therefore H_{2} = h_{2}\) (because velocity head negligibly small)

The hydraulic grade line is perfectly linear.

Q10 Comments

View/make comments; ask/answer questions. Give feedback on the questions to help us improve. Comments are anonymous.

choices.choice: The velocity into a pipe must be the same as the velocity out of the pipe
Frictional forces do not act on fluids

answers.answer: ~~~~~~~~~~Check the help box to get the answer~~~~~~~~~~
